title: FCC to end Biden-era rule that forces ISPs to list all their fees
gist: The Trump FCC under Chairman Brendan Carr will vote July 22 to scrap a Biden-era rule requiring ISPs to itemize all passthrough fees on broadband price labels, letting them show a single aggregate 'up to' amount and hide labels behind hyperlinks. The rollback favors Comcast, USTelecom and NCTA while public-interest groups warn it entrenches junk fees and kills machine-readable pricing data used for comparison shopping.

insights.claim: The FCC will vote July 22, 2026 to eliminate the requirement that ISPs itemize all passthrough fees on broadband price labels, with the order taking effect 30 days after Federal Register publication.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: A firm near-term regulatory catalyst; ISP compliance-cost relief is effectively de-risked ahead of the vote.
insights.quote: the draft order, which will be voted on at the FCC’s July 22 meeting and take effect 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register.
insights.locator: The changes have been in the works since October 2025

insights.claim: Under Chairman Brendan Carr, the Trump FCC has systematically dismantled telecom transparency requirements imposed under Democratic leadership, signaling a broader deregulatory posture toward the ISP sector.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Read as a trend: expect further consumer-protection rollbacks that reduce ISP compliance costs and regulatory overhang.
insights.quote: Under Chairman Brendan Carr, the Trump FCC has steadily whittled away at requirements imposed under Democrats.

insights.claim: The rollback originated from an October 2025 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, showing the deregulatory outcome was telegraphed nine months in advance of the vote.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The regulatory process left a long comment window; ISP lobbying inputs are on the public record for anyone tracking the pipeline.
insights.locator: The changes have been in the works since October 2025

insights.claim: ISPs routinely advertise prices well below what consumers actually pay, tacking on 'passthrough' fees they claim offset local-government charges rather than folding them into the base advertised rate.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Advertised vs. realized ARPU gap is a durable ISP revenue lever; the rule change protects that gap from scrutiny.
insights.quote: ISPs routinely advertise prices much lower than those actually charged to consumers on their monthly bills.
insights.locator: ISPs routinely advertise prices much lower

insights.claim: The new rule lets ISPs display passthrough fees as a single aggregate 'up to' maximum covering all locations, including both government fees and non-government charges like utility-pole-owner fees.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Aggregation lets ISPs obscure per-location fee variability, preserving pricing flexibility and reducing price-comparison pressure.
insights.quote: we allow providers to display such fees in the aggregate, either as a maximum or ‘up to’ amount for the total fees applicable in any location where the service plan is offered

insights.claim: The FCC defines passthrough fees as location-varying charges imposed by governments or third-party infrastructure owners that providers choose to pass through, explicitly excluding taxes; examples include right-of-way fees and pole attachment fees.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The 'anything that isn't a tax' framing shows how broad the category ISPs can now bundle into one number.
insights.locator: For purposes of this Report and Order

insights.claim: ISPs could eliminate consumer confusion by absorbing non-tax passthrough costs into advertised prices without necessarily changing what consumers ultimately pay, but choose not to.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Frames fee opacity as a deliberate marketing choice rather than a cost necessity, implicating ISP pricing conduct.
insights.quote: The prices consumers ultimately pay might not change if advertised prices were accurate
insights.locator: If ISPs wanted to make things simpler

themes.theme: Reduced label visibility and data access

insights.claim: ISPs will be allowed to replace prominent price labels on ordering pages and account portals with hyperlinks, a change the FCC concedes may result in fewer consumers reading the labels.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Lower label visibility reduces friction on ISP conversion funnels and weakens price-transparency-driven churn.
insights.quote: using hyperlinks to broadband labels instead of displaying the labels automatically may result in fewer consumers reading the label

insights.claim: The FCC will eliminate the requirement that ISPs publish price-label contents as machine-readable spreadsheet files, making it harder for third parties to scrape and aggregate broadband pricing data.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Kills a structured data source used by comparison-shopping tools and market researchers tracking broadband pricing.
insights.quote: the FCC intends to eliminate the requirement that ISPs provide the price-label contents separately in machine-readable spreadsheet files on their websites.
insights.locator: It may become more difficult for third parties

insights.claim: The FCC is relaxing phone-disclosure rules so sales reps can summarize label information conversationally instead of reciting it verbatim.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Gives ISP call centers latitude to soft-pedal fee disclosures during phone sales.
insights.quote: allow phone sales representatives to present label information conversationally, as a summary of key label fields, rather than require verbatim recitation.
insights.locator: The FCC is also relaxing the requirement

insights.claim: The FCC will scrap the requirement that ISPs archive all price labels for at least two years after a plan is discontinued, removing a historical record of how prices and services change over time.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Eliminates a longitudinal pricing dataset useful for affordability research and historical price-trend analysis.
insights.locator: Another planned change will eliminate a requirement that providers archive

insights.claim: A coalition including Public Knowledge, New America's Open Technology Institute, and the National Consumer Law Center warned the changes will worsen junk fees and hidden charges and could widen the digital divide.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
entities: Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights
insights.so_what: Organized opposition signals potential litigation or future re-regulation risk if political control shifts.
insights.quote: The Commission must not weaken oversight by allowing ISPs to operate without transparency, evade accountability, and entrench abusive practices
insights.locator: Public interest groups urged the FCC to scrap

insights.claim: Advocates compared allowing ISPs to forgo fee itemization to permitting hospitals to bill patients with no explanation of charges, medication, or facility fees.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The hospital-billing analogy frames the reputational and regulatory-reversal risk ISPs are taking on.
insights.quote: Allowing providers to forgo itemization is similar to permitting hospitals to send bills to patients with no explanation of charges, medication, or facility fees.
insights.locator: The groups said that scrapping the fee-itemization rule

insights.claim: The Utility Reform Network argued that fee itemization prevents bill shock and that an 'up to' price would only dilute the label's effectiveness and increase consumer confusion over final pricing.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Bill-shock dynamics may drive complaints and churn even as the rule shields ISPs from disclosure duties.
insights.quote: would only serve to dilute the effectiveness of the label and increase consumer confusion around how the final price they pay is calculated
insights.locator: The Utility Reform Network also said

insights.claim: USTelecom argued the itemization rule forced providers to create and update hundreds of location-specific labels to account for geographic fee variability, an outsized compliance burden.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Quantifies the compliance-cost relief lobby groups are securing; 'hundreds of labels' per provider is the burden being removed.
insights.quote: providers must create and update hundreds of different labels to account for geographic variability
insights.locator: ISPs get what they asked for

insights.claim: USTelecom dismissed machine-readable label requirements as benefiting only third-party researchers 'who are not the intended beneficiaries of the label,' framing data-mining as illegitimate.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Signals ISP intent to specifically block external pricing-transparency and comparison-shopping tools.
insights.quote: has no clear purpose or benefit except for third parties seeking to mine this information.
insights.locator: USTelecom said that requiring machine-readable information

insights.claim: Cable lobby group NCTA argued it is burdensome to maintain labels for every combination of government passthrough fees and that such transparency rules impose an outsized compliance burden without informing consumers.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Cable industry framing aligns with the FCC order, confirming the rollback is lobby-driven and durable under this administration.
insights.quote: unnecessary or unhelpful in informing consumers about the services that providers offer and impose an outsized compliance burden on providers.
insights.locator: Urging the FCC to stop requiring the listing

insights.claim: Comcast and other ISPs had earlier protested the Biden-era itemization rule specifically over the complexity of listing all the hidden fees they had chosen to charge.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Names Comcast as a lead beneficiary; the rollback directly reduces its disclosure obligations on discretionary fees.
insights.quote: The change drew protest from Comcast and other ISPs that complained bitterly about the complexity of listing all the hidden fees they had chosen to charge.
insights.locator: The Biden-era FCC updated the broadband-label rules
